content.full_text:
Re:
Farn Polrucer- PnecrrcEs Corr,rnarssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
November 2,2005
Steven S. Lucas
N'ielsen, Merksamer,
Parrinello, Mueller & Naylor, LLP
591 Redwood Highway
Mill Valley,CA 94947
Your Request for fnformal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-05-160
Dear Mr. Lucas:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Terry Mills for advice
regardil8 the post governmental employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").' Because your letter is general in nature, we are providing informal assistance.2
This letter should not be construed as advice on any conduct that may have already taken
place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political practices
Commission ("Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance.
Qn re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71; Govt. Code section 83114.)
QUESTIONS
1. Do the "permanentban" restrictions under sections 87401 and 87402 prohibit
Terry Mills a former designated ernployee of the Department of Water Resources, from
assisting his private employer in the performance of duties that were created through an
amendment to an original contract with his former state administrative agency (where
such new duties did not exist under the original contract) when the former official
participated on behalf of the state in the original contract, involving duties that have
previously been performed in full by the private employer?
t Go,re-ment Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18^997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not ,orf". the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal
written advice under section 93114. (Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)


File No. I-05-160
Page No. 2
2. Do the one year "revolving door" restrictions under section 87406(d)prohibit
Mr. Mills from providing information at a stakeholder meeting at which staff of his
former agency will be in attendance?
CONCLUSIONS
1. The permanent ban provisions under sections 87401 afi,87402 apply only to a
proceeding in which the former employee participated while in state service. An
amendment to a contract that contains new obligations that did not exist under the
original contact would not be the same proceeding. However, if the provisions of the
amended contract are substantially similar to those in the original contract when viewed
as a whole, we would consider this to be the same proceeding.
2. The one year revolving door restrictions under section 87406(d) apply to any
appearances or communications with the Department of Water Resources, or employees
thereof that are for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative action, or
any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of
a permit, license, g:ant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property, as
discussed below.
FACTS
Terry Mills is a former employee of the Califomia Department of Water
Resources ("DWR"). He retired from state service in early December 2A04 andhas
recently been employed by CH2M HILL, apivate engineering and construction
company. Several of the projects that CH2M HILL might like Mr. Mills to be involved
in are conducted under existing contracts with Mr. Mills' former ernployer, DWR.
CH2M HILL is currently performing under a contract with DWR concerning the
Upper Yuba River Studies Program ("UYRSP"). The TIYRSP is a collaborative effort
with local stakeholders to develop and perform technical studies to provide the
foundation for a recommendation to the CALFED Bay-Delta program (..CALFED,,)
regarding the feasibility of a possible fish passage project on the Yuba River. CALFED
is the largest water management and ecosystem restoration project in the nation, bringing
together 25 state and local agencies to work cooperatively with local and regional
interests to resolve often-competing demands for Califomia's limited water supply.
DWR is one of the CALFED agencies participating in the program.
The outcome of this project is a recommendation only; no permits or additional
contracts are contemplated. The project has been ongoing since 1998, under various
contracts, and has involved Mr. Mills' participation as a goverTrment employee at various
levels, as follows:
1998: The UYRSP was initiated by GALFED through contracts with the
united states Bureau of Reclamation ("USBR") and the United States
Fish and Wildlife Service.


o
File No. I-05-160
Page No. 3
1999: CHZ}i4 HILL was hired by CALFED to develop study plans;
cH2M HILL contracted through usBR to develop the study plans. Mills,
at the time an employee of the california Department of Fish and Game -
a State of california cAlFED-cooperating agency-managedthe project
for GALFED and participated in meetings with stakeholders to identifli
study needs.. Mills also led the "Agency Teaffi," a component of the
stakeholder workgroup composed of agency representatives. In this role,
he helped ensure coordination among agency participants- Mills served in
this capacity until he left CALFED in2002.
2001: CALFEDreleased a Request for Qualifications (..RFe,') for
services related to the performance of the IryRSp; Mills was involved in
preparing the solicitation on behalf of CALFED.
2002: CALFED selected 0HZM HILL through a competitive process, to
provide services for the UYRSP; Mills participated in the selection.
2002: cH2M HILL signe! a $2.1 million contract with the DwR, which
acted as the contracting agency, for the project identified in the 2001 RFe;
c}l.2}d Hill initiated work under this contract; Mills was listed as the
State's Project Manager.
2002: On behalf ofDWR and CALFED, Mills was responsible for
working with cH2M HILL on the implanentation and administration of
the TIYRSP contract.
2002: Mills left his position at cALFED and ceased his involvement in
the trYRSP, including the implementation and administration of the
CHZ}{ HILL contract, for a new position at DWR (in a different
department which is not associated with the LIyRSp).
2004: DwR signed an amendment with cH2M HILL for the LryRSp
providing for cH2M HILL to perform $2.3 million of additional work on
the UYRSP, work which was not required under the originar contract;
Mills was not involved at any level with the negotiation or execution of
the amendment, or the performance under the amended contract.
a
o
a
a
a
o
2004: In December 2004, Mills retired from state employment.
o 2005: CH2M HILL employed Mills.
As a CH2M HILL employee, Mr. Mills performs a technical role and does not
engage in direct communication with DWR. However, if permissible, Mr. Mills will
present technical information at stakeholder meetings at which DWR staff would be in
attendance. The project involves conducting technical studies and review to render a


File No. I-05-160
Page No. 4
stakeholder recommendation to the state regarding the feasibility of introducing Chinook
salmon and steelhead to the upper Yuba River.
You state that the project in which Mr. Mills would participate does not involve,
in any way, influencing administrative or legislative action, or any action or proceeding
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grarfi, or
contract, or the sale of purchase of goods or property. Mr. Mills would also not be
involved in any activities associated with performance of the original 2002 contract
entered into between CH2M HILL and DWR, the performance of which has been
completed. However, if permissible, Mr. Mills would be involved.with activities
associated with the performance of entirely new obligations created under the,2004
amendment to the contract, which were entered into between CH2MHILL and DWR
without Mr. Mills' participation.
In a recent telephone conversation, you stated that Mr. Mills has had no
involvement to date in any of the activities associated with performance of duties created
under the2004 contract amendment.
ANALYSIS
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types ofpoir
governmenta.l restrictions under the Act,r colloquially known as the "revolving door,,
prohibitions.o we discuss your questions undeieach of these provisions.
o
Permanent Ban: The first restriction is the "permanent ban" prohibiting a
former state employee from "switching sides,, and participating, for
compensation, in any specific proceeding involving the State of California or
assisting others in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former
state employee participated while employed by the state (see sections 97401-
87 402, regulation 187 41 .l).
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any judicial, quasi-judicial, or
other proceeding in whtch you parttcipated while you served as a state administrative
official. "'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding,
application, request for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim, "ontror"rry,
investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other particular matter involving a specific
party or parties in any court or state administrative agency . . . .,, (Section g7400(c),
emphasis added.)
3 In addition, section 87407 prohibits certain state and local officials from making, participating in
making, or using their official position to influence decisions affecting persons with whom tley are
negotiating employment, or has any arrangement conceming employment. (Section 874)7;regulation
18747.) However, since you have already left your state position, we need not advise regardiig this
provision.
a A comprehensive discussion of the elements of the two laws is set forth in the attached..Leaving
Your State Job? Post-Employment Restrictions May Affect You." We have attached this document for
your information. we do not repeat this information in the body of this letter.


File No. I-05-160
Page No. 5
To apply the permanent ban to your situation, you would need to identif,u the
proceedings in which Mr. Mills participated while employed by the state.s You have
stated that Mr. Mills participated on behalf of the state in the original awarding of the
contract involving his current employer. Additionally, you have indicated that in2002,
Mr. Mills "was responsible for working with CHSM HILL on the implementatton and
administration of the UYRSP contract. Therefore the permanent ban would apply to this
proceeding.
However, "the permanent ban does not aply to a 'new'proceeding even in cases
where the new proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the
official had participated. A 'new' proceeding not subject to the permanent ban typically
involves different parties, a different subject matter, or different factual issues from those
considered in previous proceedings." (Rzs/ Advice Letter,No. A-04-lg7; see also
Donovan Advice Letter, No. I-03-119.)
The issue presented in your request js whether in the performance of the
obligations created under the2O04 amendment to the contract Mr. Mills would be
participating in the same proceeding as the original contact in which Mr. Mills
participated, for purposes of the Act's post-employment provisions. In order for Mr. Mills
to represent CHZ}d HILL without violating sections 87401 and 87402, it would be
necessary to find that the amended contract constitutes a new proceeding. If so, Mr.
Mills would be able to representcHzM HILL, on the theory that he had not participated
in these "new proceedings."
In the past we have advised that if a new contract sent out for re-bid is
substantially the same as a current contract, then the two contracts will be considered the
same proceeding for purposes of the permanent ban. (Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-98-
159, enclosed.) we have also found, generally, that proceedings to draft a plan or
agreement are different from proceedings involving implementation of the same plan or
agreement, or to amend theplan or agreement.
Your facts indicate that the performance of the obligations under the original
contract has been completed, and that Mr. Mills would like to be involved on behalf of
his employer in activities associated with the performance of new obligations that were
created under the2004 amendment to the contract. Mr. Mills did not participate in the
2004 contract amendment while in state service.
You have not provided any information regarding the similarities or differences
between the original contract and the amended contract for us atalyzewhether or not
they would be considered the same proceeding. Generally, if the two contracts are
substantially similar when viewed as a whole we would consider them to be the same
s "'Participated' means to have taken part personally and substantially through decision, approval,
disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis,lnvestigation or use
of confidential information as an officer or employee, but excluding approval, disapproval oirendering of
legal advisory opinions to departmental or agency staff which do not involve u rp"iifi" part5z or parties.,,
(Section 87400 (d), regulation 18741.1.)


File No. I-05-160
Page No. 6
proceeding. (See ward Advice Letter A-05-096).6 since the outcome of this
determination is based on the provisions of the two contracts (original and amended) one
would need to examine the provisions of each contact to determine if they are essentially
the same proceeding, as we indicated in the Ward Advice Letter.
Finally, the permanent ban only applies to formal or informal appearances, and
oral or written communications with the intent to inJluence, in connectton with any
iudicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding. Since Mr. Mills would be presenting
technical information in order to render a stakeholder recommendation to the state,
presumably including his former employer- who would be in attendance at the
presentation, the appearance appears to be a communication with the intent to influence a
proceeding.
o One-Year Ban: The second restriction is the "one-year ban" prohibiting a state
employee from communicating, for compensation, with his or her former agency
for the purpose of influencing certain administrative or legislative action or
influencing certain proceedings; (See section 87 406, regulation I 87 46.1 .)
You have indicated that Mr. Mills is a former "designated employee" under the
Act, i.e., a decision-making employee whose position is designated in a state agency's
conflict of interest code, and who is required to file an annual statement of economic
interests. As a designated employee of DWR who has left state service, the Act's one-
year ban applies to him. (Section 87406(d)(I); regulation t8746.1(a)(2).)
Mr. Mills retired from state service in early December 2004. The one-year ban
will expire 12 months from his date of separation from employment with DWR. While in
effect, the one-yearban would prohibit any formal or informal appearance or oral or
written communication with his former agency for the principal pu{pose of supporting,
promoting, influencing, modiSring, opposing, delaying, or advancing the action or
proceeding.
Under regulation 18746.I, a designated employee's state administrative agency
means the agency for which he or she worked, or any board or commission under the
agency's control. The agency for which Mr. Mills worked was DWR.7 As a result, he
may not for one year, for compensation, act as representative or agent for any person
before DWR or its employees "for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative
action, or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment,
6 ln Ward, supra, we stated: "[t]he two RFPs involve the same parfy, the same subject matter, as
well as strikingly similar factual issues. Furthermore, the'new' RFP is basically the same in organization
and form to the 'draft' version, with similar (if not identical) overall goals and objectives, and is for the
purpose of procuring the same kind of service.
' Because Mr. Mills left his position with CALFED in2O02,the one-year ban, if otherwise
applicable, no longer applies to CALFED. Additionally, a former agency also includes any agency whose
budget, persorurel, and other operations are subject to the direction and control of the ageniy. We have
previously found that CALFED is not subject to the direction and control of its member agencies. (yates
Advice Letter, No. 4-00-097; Reynolds Advice Letter, No. .4.-00-250).


File No. I-05-160
Page No. 7
awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, gtant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of
goods or property." (Section s7406.) He may, however, appear before agencies other
than DWR, provided there is no prohibited conduct before an officer or employee of
DWR involved in that appearance.
An appearance or communication includes, but is not limited to, conversing by
telephone or in person, coffesponding in writing or by electronic transmission, attending
a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. In contrast to the permanent
ban, which only applies to judicial or quasi-judicial" proceedings, the one-year ban
applies to "any appearance or communication made for the purpose of influencing
administrative or legislative action,8 or influencing any action or proceeding involving
the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract,
or the sale or purchase of goods or property." Thus, if Mr. Mills is called upon to appear
or cofirmunicate with your former ageficy or agencies, the prohibition in section 87406
will apply.
Communications restricted by the one-year ban include any formal or informal
appeatance or oral or written communication made to influence legislative or
administrative action or any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment,
awarding, or revocation or a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of
goods or property. (Section 87406(dxl).) A communication is considered to be for the
purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action "if it is made for the principal
purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or
advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation rc7a6.2(a).)
Not all communications to a former state administrative agency employer are
prohibited by the one-year ban. An appearance or communication before a former state
administrative agency employer, made as part of "services performed to administer,
implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing permit, license, grmt, contract, or
sale agreement may be excluded from the [one-year] prohibitions . . . provided the
services do not involve the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of any of these
actions orproceedings." (Regulation 18746.1(b)(5XA); euiring Advice Letter, No. A-
03-272; Hanan Advice Letter, No. I-00-209.) If Mr. Mill's communications with DWR
personnel will be for the pu{pose of administering, implementing, or fulfilling the
requirements of a pre-existing contract or project involving CH2MHILL, they are not
prohibited by the one-year ban so long as the services do not involve the issuance,
amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, agreement or contract, or the
sale or purchase of goods or property and are notfor in/luencing any legislative or
adminis trative action.
8 Section 82037 defines "legislative action" as "the drafting, introduction, consideration,
modification, enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by
the Legislature or by either house or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, or by
a member or employee of the Legislature acting in his official capacity.'Legislative action' also means the
action of the Govemor in approving or vetoing any bill."


File No. I-05-160
Page No. 8
Certain other types of communications are not restricted under the one-year ban.
It is not considered a prohibited communication under the one-year ban, if an individual:
"(1) Participates as a panelist or formal speaker at a conference or
similar public event for educational purposes or to disseminate
research and the subject matter does not pertain to a specific
action or proceeding;
(2) Attends a general informational meeting, seminar, or similar
event;
(3) Requests information concerning arry matter of public record;
or
(4) Communicates with the press." (Regulation 18746.2(b)(1)-(4).)
(emphasis added.)
This is a fact-specific query. You have indicated that Mr. Mills would present
technical information at these meetings attended by DWR employees, and thatihe project
"involves conducting technical studies and review to rendeir a stakeholder
recommendation to the State regarding the feasibility of introducing Chinook salmon and
steelhead to the upper Yuba River." (Emphasis added.) To the extent that Mr. Mills
communications at these meetings involves "supporting, promoting, influencing,
modiffing, opposing, delaying, or advancing a certain action or proceeding conceming
any legislative or administrative action by DWR, the communication or appearance is
prohibited.
Finally, the Commission has advised that a former agency official may draft
proposals on a client's behalf to be submitted to the agency so long as the former
employee is not identified in connection with the client's efforts to influence
administrative action. (CookAdvice Letter, No. 4-95-321; Harrison Adviceletter, No.
A-92-289.) Similarly, the ex-employee may use his or her expertise to advise clients on
the procedural requirements, plans, or policies of the employee's former agency so long
as the anployee is not identified with the employer's efforts to influence the agency.
(Perry Advice Letter, No. 4-94-004.)
In summary, the one-yearban does not prohibit Mr. Mills from attending
meetings where DWR employees are present as long as his appearance does not involve
the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, agreement or
contract, or the sale or purchase ofgoods or property, and he does not in/luence the
agency's legislative or administrative action. The one-year ban does not prevent
Mr. Mills from advising his current employer on the project involving DWR, as long as
he is not identified in any communication with DWR and the action does not involve a
proceeding in which he participated while employed by DWR. Social conversations with
employees of DWR and other state agencies, not intended to influence administrative or
legislative action, also are not prohibited by the ban. (Tobias Advice Letter, No. 4-96-
089.) (Section 87406(dX1 ).)


File No. I-05-160
Page No. 9
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-s660
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
By:
Counsel,
vts10n
WJL:rd

sections.question:
1. Do the "permanentban" restrictions under sections 87401 and 87402 prohibit
Terry Mills a former designated ernployee of the Department of Water Resources, from
assisting his private employer in the performance of duties that were created through an
amendment to an original contract with his former state administrative agency (where
such new duties did not exist under the original contract) when the former official
participated on behalf of the state in the original contract, involving duties that have
previously been performed in full by the private employer?
t Go,re-ment Code sections 81000 - 91014. . (Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
2. Do the one year "revolving door" restrictions under section 87406(d)prohibit
Mr. Mills from providing information at a stakeholder meeting at which staff of his
former agency will be in attendance?
sections.conclusion:
1. The permanent ban provisions under sections 87401 afi,87402 apply only to a
proceeding in which the former employee participated while in state service. An
amendment to a contract that contains new obligations that did not exist under the
original contact would not be the same proceeding. However, if the provisions of the
amended contract are substantially similar to those in the original contract when viewed
as a whole, we would consider this to be the same proceeding.
2. The one year revolving door restrictions under section 87406(d) apply to any
appearances or communications with the Department of Water Resources, or employees
thereof that are for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative action, or
any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of
a permit, license, g:ant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property, as
discussed below.

participated, for purposes of the Act's post-employment provisions. In order for Mr. Mills
to represent CHZ}d HILL without violating sections 87401 and 87402, it would be
necessary to find that the amended contract constitutes a new proceeding. If so, Mr.
Mills would be able to representcHzM HILL, on the theory that he had not participated
in these "new proceedings."
In the past we have advised that if a new contract sent out for re-bid is
substantially the same as a current contract, then the two contracts will be considered the
same proceeding for purposes of the permanent ban. (Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-98-
159, enclosed.) we have also found, generally, that proceedings to draft a plan or
agreement are different from proceedings involving implementation of the same plan or
agreement, or to amend theplan or agreement.
Your facts indicate that the performance of the obligations under the original
contract has been completed, and that Mr. Mills would like to be involved on behalf of
his employer in activities associated with the performance of new obligations that were
created under the2004 amendment to the contract. Mr. Mills did not participate in the
2004 contract amendment while in state service.
You have not provided any information regarding the similarities or differences
between the original contract and the amended contract for us atalyzewhether or not
they would be considered the same proceeding. Generally, if the two contracts are
substantially similar when viewed as a whole we would consider them to be the same
s "'Participated' means to have taken part personally and substantially through decision, approval,
disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis,lnvestigation or use
of confidential information as an officer or employee, but excluding approval, disapproval oirendering of
legal advisory opinions to departmental or agency staff which do not involve u rp"iifi" part5z or parties.,,
(Section 87400 (d), regulation 18741.1.)
proceeding. (See ward Advice Letter A-05-096).6 since the outcome of this
determination is based on the provisions of the two contracts (original and amended) one
would need to examine the provisions of each contact to determine if they are essentially
the same proceeding, as we indicated in the Ward Advice Letter.
Finally, the permanent ban only applies to formal or informal appearances, and
oral or written communications with the intent to inJluence, in connectton with any
iudicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding. Since Mr. Mills would be presenting
technical information in order to render a stakeholder recommendation to the state,
presumably including his former employer- who would be in attendance at the
presentation, the appearance appears to be a communication with the intent to influence a
proceeding.
o One-Year Ban: The second restriction is the "one-year ban" prohibiting a state
employee from communicating, for compensation, with his or her former agency
for the purpose of influencing certain administrative or legislative action or
influencing certain proceedings; (See section 87 406, regulation I 87 46.1 .)
You have indicated that Mr. Mills is a former "designated employee" under the
Act, i.e., a decision-making employee whose position is designated in a state agency's
conflict of interest code, and who is required to file an annual statement of economic
interests. As a designated employee of DWR who has left state service, the Act's one-
year ban applies to him. (Section 87406(d)(I); regulation t8746.1(a)(2).)
Mr. Mills retired from state service in early December 2004. The one-year ban
will expire 12 months from his date of separation from employment with DWR. While in
effect, the one-yearban would prohibit any formal or informal appearance or oral or
written communication with his former agency for the principal pu{pose of supporting,
promoting, influencing, modiSring, opposing, delaying, or advancing the action or
proceeding.
Under regulation 18746.I, a designated employee's state administrative agency
means the agency for which he or she worked, or any board or commission under the
agency's control. The agency for which Mr. Mills worked was DWR.7 As a result, he
may not for one year, for compensation, act as representative or agent for any person
before DWR or its employees "for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative
action, or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment,
6 ln Ward, supra, we stated: "[t]he two RFPs involve the same parfy, the same subject matter, as
well as strikingly similar factual issues. Furthermore, the'new' RFP is basically the same in organization
and form to the 'draft' version, with similar (if not identical) overall goals and objectives, and is for the
purpose of procuring the same kind of service.
' Because Mr. Mills left his position with CALFED in2O02,the one-year ban, if otherwise
applicable, no longer applies to CALFED. Additionally, a former agency also includes any agency whose
budget, persorurel, and other operations are subject to the direction and control of the ageniy. We have
previously found that CALFED is not subject to the direction and control of its member agencies. (yates
Advice Letter, No. 4-00-097; Reynolds Advice Letter, No. .4.-00-250).
awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, gtant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of
goods or property." (Section s7406.) He may, however, appear before agencies other
than DWR, provided there is no prohibited conduct before an officer or employee of
DWR involved in that appearance.
An appearance or communication includes, but is not limited to, conversing by
telephone or in person, coffesponding in writing or by electronic transmission, attending
a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. In contrast to the permanent
ban, which only applies to judicial or quasi-judicial" proceedings, the one-year ban
applies to "any appearance or communication made for the purpose of influencing
administrative or legislative action,8 or influencing any action or proceeding involving
the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract,
or the sale or purchase of goods or property." Thus, if Mr. Mills is called upon to appear
or cofirmunicate with your former ageficy or agencies, the prohibition in section 87406
will apply.
Communications restricted by the one-year ban include any formal or informal
appeatance or oral or written communication made to influence legislative or
administrative action or any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment,
awarding, or revocation or a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of
goods or property. (Section 87406(dxl).) A communication is considered to be for the
purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action "if it is made for the principal
purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or
advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation rc7a6.2(a).)
Not all communications to a former state administrative agency employer are
prohibited by the one-year ban. An appearance or communication before a former state
administrative agency employer, made as part of "services performed to administer,
implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing permit, license, grmt, contract, or
sale agreement may be excluded from the [one-year] prohibitions . . . provided the
services do not involve the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of any of these
actions orproceedings." (Regulation 18746.1(b)(5XA); euiring Advice Letter, No. A-
03-272; Hanan Advice Letter, No. I-00-209.) If Mr. Mill's communications with DWR
personnel will be for the pu{pose of administering, implementing, or fulfilling the
requirements of a pre-existing contract or project involving CH2MHILL, they are not
prohibited by the one-year ban so long as the services do not involve the issuance,
amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, agreement or contract, or the
sale or purchase of goods or property and are notfor in/luencing any legislative or
adminis trative action.
8 Section 82037 defines "legislative action" as "the drafting, introduction, consideration,
modification, enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by
the Legislature or by either house or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, or by
a member or employee of the Legislature acting in his official capacity.'Legislative action' also means the
action of the Govemor in approving or vetoing any bill."
Certain other types of communications are not restricted under the one-year ban.
It is not considered a prohibited communication under the one-year ban, if an individual:
"(1) Participates as a panelist or formal speaker at a conference or
similar public event for educational purposes or to disseminate
research and the subject matter does not pertain to a specific
action or proceeding;
(2) Attends a general informational meeting, seminar, or similar
event;
(3) Requests information concerning arry matter of public record;
or
(4) Communicates with the press." (Regulation 18746.2(b)(1)-(4).)
(emphasis added.)
This is a fact-specific query. You have indicated that Mr. Mills would present
technical information at these meetings attended by DWR employees, and thatihe project
"involves conducting technical studies and review to rendeir a stakeholder
recommendation to the State regarding the feasibility of introducing Chinook salmon and
steelhead to the upper Yuba River." (Emphasis added.) To the extent that Mr. Mills
communications at these meetings involves "supporting, promoting, influencing,
modiffing, opposing, delaying, or advancing a certain action or proceeding conceming
any legislative or administrative action by DWR, the communication or appearance is
prohibited.
Finally, the Commission has advised that a former agency official may draft
proposals on a client's behalf to be submitted to the agency so long as the former
employee is not identified in connection with the client's efforts to influence
administrative action. (CookAdvice Letter, No. 4-95-321; Harrison Adviceletter, No.
A-92-289.) Similarly, the ex-employee may use his or her expertise to advise clients on
the procedural requirements, plans, or policies of the employee's former agency so long
as the anployee is not identified with the employer's efforts to influence the agency.
(Perry Advice Letter, No. 4-94-004.)
In summary, the one-yearban does not prohibit Mr. Mills from attending
meetings where DWR employees are present as long as his appearance does not involve
the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, agreement or
contract, or the sale or purchase ofgoods or property, and he does not in/luence the
agency's legislative or administrative action. The one-year ban does not prevent
Mr. Mills from advising his current employer on the project involving DWR, as long as
he is not identified in any communication with DWR and the action does not involve a
proceeding in which he participated while employed by DWR. Social conversations with
employees of DWR and other state agencies, not intended to influence administrative or
legislative action, also are not prohibited by the ban. (Tobias Advice Letter, No. 4-96-
089.) (Section 87406(dX1 ).)

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Do the "permanentban" restrictions under sections 87401 and 87402 prohibit
Terry Mills a former designated ernployee of the Department of Water Resources, from
assisting his private employer in the performance of duties that were created through an
amendment to an original contract with his former state administrative agency (where
such new duties did not exist under the original contract) when the former official
participated on behalf of the state in the original contract, involving duties that have
previously been performed in full by the private employer?
t Go,re-ment Code sections 81000 - 91014. . (Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
2. Do the one year "revolving door" restrictions under section 87406(d)prohibit
Mr. Mills from providing information at a stakeholder meeting at which staff of his
former agency will be in attendance?

CONCLUSION: 1. The permanent ban provisions under sections 87401 afi,87402 apply only to a
proceeding in which the former employee participated while in state service. An
amendment to a contract that contains new obligations that did not exist under the
original contact would not be the same proceeding. However, if the provisions of the
amended contract are substantially similar to those in the original contract when viewed
as a whole, we would consider this to be the same proceeding.
2. The one year revolving door restrictions under section 87406(d) apply to any
appearances or communications with the Department of Water Resources, or employees
thereof that are for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative action, or
any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of
a permit, license, g:ant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property, as
discussed below.

Lucas N'ielsen, Merksamer, Parrinello, Mueller & Naylor, LLP 591 Redwood Highway Mill Valley,CA 94947 Your Request for fnformal Assistance Our FiIe No. I-05-160 Dear Mr. Lucas: This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Terry Mills for advice regardil8 the post governmental employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' Because your letter is general in nature, we are providing informal assistance.2 This letter should not be construed as advice on any conduct that may have already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political practices Commission ("Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. Qn re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71; Govt. Code section 83114.) QUESTIONS 1. Do the "permanentban" restrictions under sections 87401 and 87402 prohibit Terry Mills a former designated ernployee of the Department of Water Resources, from assisting his private employer in the performance of duties that were created through an amendment to an original contract with his former state administrative agency (where such new duties did not exist under the original contract) when the former official participated on behalf of the state in the original contract, involving duties that have previously been performed in full by the private employer? t Go,re-ment Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18^997, of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not ,orf". the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal written advice under section 93114. (Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.) File No. I-05-160 Page No. 2 2. Do the one year "revolving door" restrictions under section 87406(d)prohibit Mr. Mills from providing information at a stakeholder meeting at which staff of his former agency will be in attendance? CONCLUSIONS 1. The permanent ban provisions under sections 87401 afi,87402 apply only to a proceeding in which the former employee participated while in state service. An amendment to a contract that contains new obligations that did not exist under the original contact would not be the same proceeding. However, if the provisions of the amended contract are substantially similar to those in the original contract when viewed as a whole, we would consider this to be the same proceeding. 2. The one year revolving door restrictions under section 87406(d) apply to any appearances or communications with the Department of Water Resources, or employees thereof that are for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative action, or any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, g:ant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property, as discussed below. FACTS Terry Mills is a former employee of the Califomia Department of Water Resources ("DWR"). He retired from state service in early December 2A04 andhas recently been employed by CH2M HILL, apivate engineering and construction
